coredata.dc:title: Optimized parameter estimation of lithium-ion batteries using an improved cuckoo search algorithm under variable temperature profile
coredata.prism:publicationName: e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Dar, Tasadeek Hassan
Singh, Satyavir
coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries are an intuitive choice for electric vehicles and many other gadgets. Parameters play a critical role in addressing its performance characterization. Accurate parameter estimation and real-time monitoring of lithium-ion batteries are important in modeling equivalent circuits. The characteristics of lithium-ion batteries are dynamic due to energy storage. Dynamical behavior is characterized by RC equivalent models. This work presents the estimation of parameters associated with the n-RC equivalent circuit model in integration with the Improved Cuckoo Search Algorithm (ICSA). To get it, battery tests such as HPPC test, static capacity test, and open circuit voltage test in consideration of temperatures are carried out. The experiments are carried out under different temperature ranges to record the valid data sets. ICSA is advantageous over existing algorithms in estimating the battery parameters under temperature ranges. The performance of the proposed approach captures and estimates the parameters in the dynamic range of temperatures of the lithium-ion battery. The error profile is addressed with the root mean square error and it is found to be 0.23 % at 30 °C. It is observed that experimental data with ICSA accurately matches the simulated model data at different temperature ranges.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Battery management system
HPPC test
Lithium-ion battery
Parameter estimation
Improved cuckoo search algorithm

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy

voltage and current discharge waveforms of HPPC test at variable temperatures where the left Y axis represents current and the right Y axis represents voltage.
Flowchart of Improved Cuckoo Search Algorithm.

simulated and measured single pulse voltages before and after optimization at different temperatures. (i) 25 °C; (ii) 50 °C; (iii) 25 °C; (iv) 50 °C.
(i)–(iv) Measured versus simulated voltage after ICSA approach and (v)-(viii) error distribution between measured and simulated voltage.
Error comparison between the measured and simulated voltage results at four different temperatures.
Comparison between the proposed approach and the statistical results from different estimation techniques at 25 °C.

head.ce:title: Optimized parameter estimation of lithium-ion batteries using an improved cuckoo search algorithm under variable temperature profile

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, SRM University AP, Andhra Pradesh, 522240, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering, SRM University AP, Andhra Pradesh, 522240, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries are an intuitive choice for electric vehicles and many other gadgets. Parameters play a critical role in addressing its performance characterization. Accurate parameter estimation and real-time monitoring of lithium-ion batteries are important in modeling equivalent circuits. The characteristics of lithium-ion batteries are dynamic due to energy storage. Dynamical behavior is characterized by RC equivalent models. This work presents the estimation of parameters associated with the n-RC equivalent circuit model in integration with the Improved Cuckoo Search Algorithm (ICSA). To get it, battery tests such as HPPC test, static capacity test, and open circuit voltage test in consideration of temperatures are carried out. The experiments are carried out under different temperature ranges to record the valid data sets. ICSA is advantageous over existing algorithms in estimating the battery parameters under temperature ranges. The performance of the proposed approach captures and estimates the parameters in the dynamic range of temperatures of the lithium-ion battery. The error profile is addressed with the root mean square error and it is found to be 0.23 % at 30 °C. It is observed that experimental data with ICSA accurately matches the simulated model data at different temperature ranges.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium-ion batteries are the epitome source of energy storage solutions for the broader area of applications including electronic gadgets, electric vehicles (EVs), and renewable energy storage systems. Lithium-ion battery performance is affected by capacity, state of charge (SOC), and internal parameters such as resistance and capacitance [1]. To improve the strategies related to battery management, the estimation accuracy of these parameters is essential for enhancing the operating efficiency of the system [2,3].

The primary characteristic affecting the performance of the battery is temperature. Under different temperature ranges, lithium-ion batteries behave differently hence impacting the performance of the overall system [4]. Due to change in temperature, internal parameters such as capacitance, resistance, conductivity, and reactions happening inside the battery exhibit different behavior. It is compulsory to analyze the changes happening inside the battery in charge transfer and diffusion polarization under different temperature ranges to develop a system that can work in variable environmental conditions [5,6].

Lithium-ion batteries usually overheat while charging and discharging at high current rates and can impact the reaction process inside the battery leading to the production of more heat which can cause thermal runaway [7]. Therefore, studying the thermal characteristics of the battery at various temperatures under different current rates for charging and discharging is essential [8]. Literature studies in the field of battery management have introduced different kinds of models that possess various complexities for tracing the parameters. Three such models include mathematical, electrochemical, and equivalent circuit models [9]. Electrochemical models are more accurate but come with complexity and high time consumption due to the incorporation of partial differential equations [10]. Mathematical models are easy and simple to implement but because of the nonlinear behaviour of lithium-ion batteries, these models are subjected to inaccuracies. Therefore, it becomes essential to use a model that has enough ability to depict the behaviour of the battery in its simplest form. Equivalent circuit models (ECM) have become popular due to their less complexity and real-time estimation abilities [11,12]. There are various RC models presented in the literature such as the Rint model, one RC model, and Two RC models. The two RC model is commonly used, as the number of RC branches increases accuracy of the model increases but adds more complexity, and with a smaller number of RC branches decreases accuracy but is simple to implement. To balance the complexity and accuracy two RC models are preferred commonly under such a scenario [13,14].

Second-order equivalent circuit model developed for lithium titanate oxide battery. The model was validated through simulation and comparison with laboratory measurements. Methods used were the current pulse process for battery model parameterization and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy for parameterizing impedance-based electrical models [15]. The coevolutionary particle swarm optimization (CPSO) method identifies battery parameters using real-time data for optimization, ECM parameters optimized by separate parameter particle swarm (PPS) to accurately verify results and PPS optimizes parameters with <5 mV deviation [16]. A recursive least squares (RLS) approach frequently employs a forgetting strategy to lower the lag residual and enhance its identification to time-varying parameters. [17] presented a method that minimizes model identification bias caused by noise effectively integrating the state of charge estimation and recursive total least squares (RTLS) has low computational complexity compared to other methods. [18] introduced the IA-MAFF-RLS method for online lithium-ion battery parameter identification and achieved the lowest average mean square deviation (MSD) of −20.17 dB from benchmark methods. To prevent parameter outbreak insignificant levels of excitation, [19] proposes a direct forgetting approach that was implemented through an AFF-RLS algorithm, when no information was lost while in an inactive input domain. [20,21] proposed a multiple AFFRLS approach to produce the coefficients of regression reflecting various time-varying properties. however, the operational effectiveness of such a method is hampered by the fact that some of these coefficients are difficult to alter and have no physical value.

Numerous research works have investigated the temperature-dependent parameter estimation of lithium-ion batteries. Both [22] and [23] put forth strategies for precise SOC estimation; [22] concentrated on a Thevenin equivalent circuit model, while [23] focused on an enhanced equivalent circuit model. Improved SOC estimation accuracy at different temperatures was shown in both cases. To describe the consideration of temperature variations in battery parameters, [24] introduced adaptive parameter identification approaches and compensation strategies, further improving existing methods. Temperature fluctuations have a considerable impact on the performance and aging of lithium-ion batteries. Battery properties like internal resistance and capacitance vary nonlinearly with temperature. The aforementioned parameter estimation approaches frequently fail to account for these temperature fluctuations, causing inaccuracies in simulation and prediction. Other advanced optimization approaches, including genetic algorithms (GA), ant colony optimization (ACO), and differential evolution (DE), have been used to estimate parameters [16]. However, the cuckoo search algorithm (CSA) stands out for its simplicity, fewer tuning options, and effective balance of exploration and exploitation [25,26]. The findings from this research emphasize how crucial precise parameter estimates are for lithium-ion batteries, especially when temperature variations are involved.

So, to highlight the variation of parameters with the variable range of temperatures, this work proposes a novel method of the Improved Cuckoo Search Algorithm (ICSA) integrated with a second-order RC equivalent circuit model to identify the parameters of lithium-ion batteries under different temperature tests. Several approaches have been adopted to estimate the offline parameters of the batteries, though the CSA has proved superior among all other algorithms mentioned in the literature. It is observed that traditional CSA has low calculation accuracy with repetition of the identified parameters and convergence rate is slow. This work proposes ICSA to overcome the limitation of CSA in parameter estimation, by variable adjustment of probability factor, step size control, and exponential curves for the parameters under observation. To trace the non-linearities occurring during the temperature change ICSA is employed to estimate the internal parameters precisely with the reduction of the error between simulated and measured voltage waveforms. The small variations in parameter estimation with temperature change are traced and computationally simulated for better performance of the battery system.

ce:list-item.ce:para: By accounting for the battery dynamics with various time constants, increasing the ECM order typically increases modeling accuracy. However, higher order imposes a high computational burden. In an ECM, the compromise between the precision and complexity of the model must be made because microprocessors in battery management systems (BMS) for EVs have limited processing capacity. To balance between the complexity and accuracy two RC equivalent models are taken into account [27]. Fig. 1 represents the two RC equivalent circuit models of the battery. The open circuit voltage is denoted by Vocv (SOC). R0, R1, and R2 denoted the resistances, and C1 and C2 show the capacitances of the battery. Here, R1 and C1 represent the polarization process, and R2 and C2 bring the effect of the diffusion process. Vt is terminal voltage, and I stand for the current drawn from the battery. The battery characteristics are shown electrically as:

ce:section.ce:para:
Apply Kirchhoff's law, the voltage across the circuit is given as [28]

( t )

The proposed framework is given in Fig. 2 which describes the procedural steps of the experimental and simulation to be carried out. To estimate the parameters of the lithium-ion batteries, a list of experiments is needed such as the HPPC test, open circuit voltage test, and capacity test. The fundamental setup for such tests is a battery cycler, data acquisition device, programmable software, and a temperature chamber to meet the environmental conditions [30]. The testing platform of battery is presented in Fig. 3 which shows the visualization of the components involved in testing and data acquisition of the lithium-ion battery in a detailed manner. The battery cycler used has a peak limit voltage of 20 V and a peak limit current of 20 A. The software installed on the host computer is Neware (BTS-4000), which programs the test conditions and performs the acquisition of data from the battery. The temperature chamber shown in Fig. 3 is used to carry out experiments at a variable range of temperatures. The tests conducted on the battery under four temperatures are 25, 30,40, and 50 °C. The specifications of the battery used are NCA (nickel cobalt Aluminium)−18,650 3000mAh lithium-ion battery has a voltage rating of 4.2 V, 3000 mAh capacity, a 3 C limit of continuous discharge current, and a 1 C limit of continuous charging current. The dimensions of the battery utilized is 18.33 ± 0.07, 64.85 ± 0.15 (dia & height in mm). An experimental setup to test the battery was held under a controlled environment with a controlled thermal chamber at desired temperatures. The purpose of the capacity test is to ascertain the true capacity of lithium-ion battery, which is crucial to comprehending its potential for energy storage.

This test is the main component in battery management systems, especially in the domain of electric vehicles. In [31] and [32] suggested that the OCV-SOC test is more reliable and accurate to trace the state of charge and other parameters of any battery, focusing on the relaxation period. The lower temperature (below 25 °C) study is exempted from this work due to the unavailability of equipment used for testing at lower temperatures. Four temperature considerations have been taken into account including 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C. The procedure to carry out the open circuit voltage test is shown in Fig. 4 in a detailed manner. The OCV-SOC relation at different temperatures is discussed in the result section.

This test is carried out to determine the behavior of lithium-ion batteries under different load conditions to depict the dynamic response and cell capabilities. The HPPC test is used to determine the abrupt voltage variations due to load change while sustaining stability [33]. The main aim of this test is to provide insights about the real-time variation of the voltage subjected to different temperature and load conditions which adds to understanding the internal parameters and dynamic response of the battery. The test is conducted for different temperatures such as 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C. The procedure to conduct the HPPC test for the battery is shown in Fig. 5 . To conduct this test the battery should be fully charged by the constant voltage constant current method (CCCV) followed by the pulses given at a current rate of 1.5 C [34]. Pulses should be given for a period of 9 s calculated from the discharging time of the battery. The test should be followed by the 10 % capacity removal of the remaining capacity, these steps need to be repeated until the 0 % SOC is reached. The discharge Voltage and discharge Current profiles obtained through the HPPC test are represented in Fig. 6 , as the experiment is conducted for four temperatures the voltage and current profile of four different temperatures are shown in Fig. 6.

The ICSA algorithm is implemented to optimize each pulse of the HPPC test to accurately estimate the parameters. It begins by loading a group of N cuckoos inside the problem's search parameters. Determining the global and local hunt state is the second stage in this approach. The idea of population and generational division is used in both of these phases to identify a global solution. In order to diversify the output during the beginning population set and eventually converge them during the subsequent stages toward a global outcome, the idea of population division has been incorporated. On the other hand, the division of iterations is also used to preserve equilibrium between the local and global search states. The ICSA flowchart process is explained in the flowchart given in Fig. 7 . The fundamental ideas of Cuckoo Search are applied in the initial part of the iterations of the global search phase. Here, Cauchy-based search (CS) is used instead of Lévy flight-based random search [35]. The global search phase for CS's general search equation is provided by
(10)
X i
t + 1
=
X i t
+ α ⊗ c a u c h y
( δ )
(
X i t
−
X r t
)
where δ is the Lévy assigned random number, α > 0 is the step size, and
X i t
is the current estimate of parameters. The present best parameter is represented by
X r t
.
The objective function minimizes the error between simulated and measured terminal voltage by mean square error (MSE) and is given as:
(11)
MSE =
∑
k = 1
n
(
V
m e a s
, k
−
V
s i m
, k
)
2
Where
V
m e a s
, k
is the measured voltage and
V
s i m
, k
is the simulated voltage at the kth iteration.
The search equation for local random walks of Cuckoo search is utilized for the local search phase and can be found as follows:
(12)
X i
t + 1
=
X i t
+ α ⊗ H
(
p − ϵ
)
⊗
(
X g t
−
X h t
)
where ⊗ entrywise multiplication, ϵ is a random variable, p is the probability taken as 0.75, and
X g t
,
X h t
are random outcomes taken from the defined population.
The application of four search equations to create a new outcome is employed in the local search state. The first search equation, Eq. (12), is the same as the one employed by the Cuckoo Search algorithm, whereas the second is given as:
(13)
X i t
=
X i t
+ F .
{
(
X j t
−
X k t
)
+
(
X l t
−
X m t
)
}
The last search equation for the population's component follows as:
(14)
X i
t + 1
=
X i t
+ a .
(
X
b e s t
t
−
X j t
)
+ b .
(
X k t
−
X l t
)
where a and b are randomly distributed numbers with uniform distribution inside the interval [0, 1]. This search equation is mostly used because, while two other solutions assist in going toward the global solution, the optimal solution will direct one random result.
The probability factor is chosen by implementing an exponentially declining function to make a trade-off between the exploitation and exploration elements, and it is given by
(15)
p
( t )
=
p
m i n
+
(
p
m a x
−

)
.

−
(
t
t

/ 10
)
where t is the present iteration, e is the exponential function, and
t
m a x
is the total number of iterations taken as 200.
p
m a x
a n d
p
m i n
are taken within the interval {0,1}. The exponentially declining function is used because, in the early iterations, it helps the algorithm converge slowly and change more quickly in the later stages. This aids the algorithm even more in keeping the global and local search phases in balance. In addition, the population size is decreased by one-fourth if the total number of iterations exceeds three-fourths of the total iteration size. Resulted solution was associated with fewer function evaluations and solutions after iterations [36].

OCV is the most common and important parameter for any battery, and it varies with SOC. The relation between the two is commonly linear, but not in all types of batteries. The lithium-ion batteries are an exception as they follow nonlinearity which can be determined by the experimental procedure explained in Fig. 4. The data obtained is fitted by the implementation of a polynomial fitting function [37,38]. The graphical relation of OCV with SOC is illustrated in Fig. 8 under the variable range of temperatures. The expressions shown in Eqs. (16)–(19) are the polynomial equations for the OCV-SOC curve at temperatures 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C respectively. The OCV-SOC curve is determined by the 6th-order polynomial function.
(16)
O C V = − 23.459
z
6
+ 76.278
z
5
− 96.73
z

+ 59.893
z

− 18.76

+ 3.79
z +
3.154

O C V =
3.382

O C V =
1.495

2.992

Ohmic resistance can be calculated either during discharge or at the relaxation point. Fig. 10 shows the single discharge pulse extracted from the test data. The points named are P1P2, P2P3, P3P4, and P4P5. Points P1P2 and P3P4 represent the rapid drop voltage because of load discharge from the no-load moment. This rapid drop is because of the resistance R0 whereas points P2P3 and P4P5 voltage drop are because of the capacitors present in the network, it represents the discharging of the battery in a gradual manner. Ohmic resistance can be calculated from sudden drop at discharge points P1 to P2 as shown in Fig. 9 and calculated by Eq. (20). P1 is the OCV point which will be known, P2 must be noted down, and pulse current will be used to identify the parameter R0. Eq. (20) shows the calculation of ohmic resistance according to Ohm's law. I is the current flowing through the battery. Fig. 10(i) shows the change in ohmic resistance with respect to SOC at different temperatures. This method is applied to all the discharge pulses under different ranges of temperatures. The characteristics curve showed us a negative non-linear relationship between the SOC and the ohmic resistance, R0 . It demonstrates that the optimal R
0
value for SOC levels varies between 20 % and 80 %. This also shows how the mass movement of ions causes the R0 value may increase in the lowest and highest SOC regions.
(20)
R 0
=
P 1
−
P 2
I

To identify remaining parameters are calculated using the Eqs. (5), (6), and (7). Since the curve of the voltage during discharge and relaxation periods depends on the two RC pairs connected in series, it is difficult to segregate them. It is difficult to identify the periods responsible for transient and steady states by visualization, so it is important to identify both voltage variations altogether [15]. The RC parameters (R1, C1, R2, and C2) are identified and stored in the lookup table form for the entire discharge cycle of the battery. The parameters are shown in Fig. 10 under four different temperatures.
τ 1
and
τ 2
are time constants of the circuit that altogether represent the total time constant of the circuit. The variation of time constants is illustrated in Fig. 10(vi) and (vii) at different temperatures. Fig. 10(ii) and (iii) show the variation of the resistance of the battery under different temperatures at variable SOC ranges and Fig. 10(iv) and (v) show the variation of capacitance under different temperatures at variable ranges of SOC. From the parameter results, the variation of all the parameters is visible which shows the change in temperature affects the battery dynamics drastically. From Fig. 10 it is shown that with the increment in temperature, the resistance reduces due to enhanced ionic mobility while as the capacitance is slightly increased due to better reaction kinetics.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
To obtain the precise linearized interpolation values of the parameters, the resulting parameters are fed into the MATLAB-developed Simulink model of the second-order equivalent circuit in the form of lookup tables. The model development shows the behavior of battery dynamics under different temperature conditions. Simulink model of second order equivalent circuit is illustrated in Fig. 11 . The measured and simulated voltage mismatch error is termed a modeling error. The ICSA is implemented to reduce the same error at each discharge pulse. By the ICSA approach, the RMS error is minimized to approximately 0.26 % and calculated by Eq. (21).
(21)
RMS
Error
=
1 n
∑
k = 1
n
(
V
m k
−
V
s k
)
2
× 100
%

For n-voltage pulses, the error is recorded for the kth instant, k = 1,2……, n, and is given as a percentage. Where
V
s k
is the simulated battery voltage from the ECM model at the kth instant, and
V
m k
is the voltage measured during the experiment.

ce:section.ce:para: ICSA Optimization approach is implemented to optimize the simulated output from the battery with respect to the experimental voltage. The single voltage pulses at each temperature shown in Fig. 12 are displayed before optimization and after applying the ICSA approach where Fig. 12(i)–(iv) is at 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C temperatures respectively. Only single pulses at 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C each are shown here, however, it's not feasible to show all the pulses under all four temperatures due to space constraints. Results of the HPPC test voltage waveforms after optimization which almost show the overlap of measured and simulated voltage validate the model with acceptable accuracy. The terminal voltage for 25, 30, 40, and 50 °C temperature is shown in Fig. 13 (i)–(iv) respectively and the corresponding error voltage distributions are illustrated by histograms in Fig. 13(v)–(viii). Table 1 shows the error voltage comparison between different temperatures where the algorithm shows better performance at 30 °C with an RMSE error percentage of 0.23 %. Table 2 presents a comparison of the statistical outcomes of the model parameter estimation techniques. The proposed method shows the RMSE error value of 0.26 % at 25 °C. These findings suggest that the proposed approach is performing better at estimating the parameters of LIBs over the existing techniques.

The study discussed the conduction of OCV and HPPC tests at various temperatures to capture the operating data of the battery and from such experimental data, it is concluded that under different temperatures, the battery behaviour is affected. Parameters are identified under different temperatures with ICSA and the effect of temperature on each parameter has been examined. The modeling error between the proposed measurement and simulated voltage is reduced to an RMSE magnitude of 0.23 % at the 30 °C. The error distribution of the simulated and measured voltage is analysed and concluded that with temperature the nonlinearities are increased in the battery operation. This work presents an efficient technique to capture the battery parameters under ranges of temperature and shows better performance as compared to existing parameter estimation techniques. Further, results presented show higher accuracy with the lowest complexity in the estimated parameters, simplifying the complexity of battery management systems and makes it effective for electric vehicle applications. Future work will be done to reduce the computation time in the proposed technique due to the optimization of every discharge pulse separately.

Tasadeek Hassan Dar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Satyavir Singh: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Amara Raja Centre SRM University AP for giving the platform for battery testing to carry out this research.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A hierarchical adaptive extended Kalman filter algorithm for lithium-ion battery state of charge estimation
J. Energy Storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Wang, Y. Yang, and T. Gu, “A hierarchical adaptive extended Kalman filter algorithm for lithium-ion battery state of charge estimation,” J. Energy Storage, vol. 62, Jun. 2023, doi: 10.1016/j.est.2023.106831.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced extended-input LSTM with an adaptive singular value decomposition UKF for LIB SOC estimation using full-cycle current rate and temperature data
Appl. Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Takyi-Aninakwa et al., “Enhanced extended-input LSTM with an adaptive singular value decomposition UKF for LIB SOC estimation using full-cycle current rate and temperature data,” Appl. Energy, vol. 363, Jun. 2024, doi: 10.1016/j.apenergy.2024.123056.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Parameter estimation of a lithium-ion battery equivalent circuit model using a pseudo-random impulse sequence perturbation
2023 IEEE International Conference on Environment and Electrical Engineering and 2023 IEEE Industrial and Commercial Power Systems Europe (EEEIC /I&CPS Europe)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.C. Moyo and F.M. Mwaniki, “Parameter estimation of a lithium-ion battery equivalent circuit model using a pseudo-random impulse sequence perturbation,” in 2023 IEEE International Conference on Environment and Electrical Engineering and 2023 IEEE Industrial and Commercial Power Systems Europe (EEEIC /I&CPS Europe), IEEE, Jun. 2023, pp. 1–6. doi: 10.1109/EEEIC/ICPSEurope57605.2023.10194865.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tasadeek Hassan Dar was born in Kashmir, India, in March 1997. He received a B.E. degree in Electrical and Electronics Engineering from rajarajeswari College of Engineering Bengaluru, India, and M tech degree in Electrical Engineering from Tripura University, Tripura, India. He is currently pursuing a Ph.D. degree in Electrical Engineering with SRM University Andhra Pradesh, India.
His research interests include modeling and parameter estimation of batteries and data-driven techniques to estimate the state of charge for electric vehicles.

Satyavir Singh received the BTech in Electrical Engineering from Uttar Pradesh Technical University Lucknow, India, in 2005 and ME degree from Madhav Institute of Technology and Science Gwalior, India, in 2008 and the Ph.D. degree from National Institute of Technology Srinagar, India, in 2020.

From 2008 to 2010, he was a Senior Lecturer at Aryan Institute of Technology Ghaziabad, India. He has been an Assistant Professor 2010 to 2015 at Hindustan College of Science & Technology Mathura, India. Then he joined Bajaj Institute of Technology Wardha, India in 2019 to 2022 as an Assistant Professor. He is currently working at SRM University AP, India. His research interests include Electric vehicles, battery modelling, energy storage technology and battery state estimation.
